**New Spain**, officially the **Viceroyalty of New Spain** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Virreinato de Nueva España* [[birejˈnato ðe ˈnweβa esˈpaɲa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"); [Nahuatl languages](/wiki/Nahuatl_languages "Nahuatl languages"): *Yankwik Kaxtillan Birreiyotl*),[[7]](#cite_note-9) originally the **Kingdom of New Spain**, was an integral territorial entity of the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), established by [Habsburg Spain](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain"). It was one of several domains established during the [Spanish conquest of the Americas](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas"), and had its capital in Mexico City. Its jurisdiction comprised a large area of the southern and western portions of North America, mainly what became Mexico and the Southwestern United States, but also [California](/wiki/California "California"), [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") and [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_Spain) "Louisiana (New Spain)"); Central America (as part of [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico")); the Caribbean like [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola") and [Martinique](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique"), and northern parts of South America, even [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"); several Pacific archipelagos, including the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam"). Additional Asian colonies included "[Spanish Formosa](/wiki/Spanish_Formosa "Spanish Formosa")", on the island of [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").

After the 1521 [Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Aztec_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire"), conqueror [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés") named the territory New Spain, and established the new capital, Mexico City, on the site of [Tenochtitlan](/wiki/Tenochtitlan "Tenochtitlan"), the capital of the [Aztec Empire](/wiki/Aztec_Empire "Aztec Empire"). Central Mexico became the base of expeditions of exploration and conquest, expanding the territory claimed by the Spanish Empire. With the political and economic importance of the conquest, the crown asserted direct control over the densely populated realm. The crown established New Spain as a [viceroyalty](/wiki/Viceroyalty "Viceroyalty") in 1535, appointing as viceroy [Antonio de Mendoza](/wiki/Antonio_de_Mendoza "Antonio de Mendoza"), an aristocrat loyal to the monarch rather than the conqueror Cortés. New Spain was the first of the viceroyalties that Spain created, the second being [Peru](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_Peru "Viceroyalty of Peru") in 1542, following the [Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Inca_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire"). Both New Spain and Peru had dense indigenous populations at conquest as a source of labor and material wealth in the form of vast silver deposits, discovered and exploited beginning in the mid-1600s.

New Spain developed strong regional divisions based on local climate, topography, distance from the capital and the Gulf Coast port of [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz_(city) "Veracruz (city)"), size and complexity of indigenous populations, and the presence or absence of mineral resources. Central and southern Mexico had dense indigenous populations, each with complex social, political, and economic organization, but no large-scale deposits of silver to draw Spanish settlers. By contrast, the northern area of Mexico was arid and mountainous, a region of nomadic and semi-nomadic indigenous populations, which do not easily support human settlement. In the 1540s, the discovery of [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver") in [Zacatecas](/wiki/Zacatecas "Zacatecas") attracted Spanish mining entrepreneurs and workers, to exploit the mines, as well as crown officials to ensure the crown received its share of revenue. Silver mining became integral not only to the development of New Spain, but also to the enrichment of the Spanish crown, which marked [a transformation in the global economy](/wiki/Global_silver_trade_from_the_16th_to_19th_centuries "Global silver trade from the 16th to 19th centuries"). New Spain's port of [Acapulco](/wiki/Acapulco "Acapulco") became the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") terminus of the transpacific trade with the Philippines via the [Manila galleon](/wiki/Manila_galleon "Manila galleon"). New Spain became a vital link between Spain's New World empire and [its East Indies empire](/wiki/Spanish_East_Indies "Spanish East Indies").

From the beginning of the 19th century, the kingdom fell into crisis, aggravated by the 1808 [Napoleonic invasion of Iberia](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") and the forced abdication of the Bourbon monarch, [Charles IV](/wiki/Charles_IV_of_Spain "Charles IV of Spain"). This resulted in a political crisis in New Spain and much of the Spanish Empire in 1808, which ended with the government of Viceroy [José de Iturrigaray](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_Iturrigaray "José de Iturrigaray"). Conspiracies of American-born Spaniards sought to take power, leading to the [Mexican War of Independence](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence"), 1810–1821. At its conclusion in 1821, the viceroyalty was dissolved and the [Mexican Empire](/wiki/First_Mexican_Empire "First Mexican Empire") was established. Former royalist military officer turned insurgent for independence [Agustín de Iturbide](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_de_Iturbide "Agustín de Iturbide") would be crowned as emperor.

## The Crown and the Viceroyalty of New Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: The Crown and the Viceroyalty of New Spain")]

The Kingdom of New Spain was established on 18 August 1521, following the [Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Aztec_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire"), as a New World kingdom ruled by the [Crown of Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile"). The initial funds for exploration came from [Queen Isabella](/wiki/Isabella_I_of_Castile "Isabella I of Castile").[[8]](#cite_note-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11) Although New Spain was a dependency of Castile, it (Mexico) was a kingdom and not a colony, subject to the presiding monarch on the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula").[[10]](#cite_note-Haring_7-12)[[11]](#cite_note-13)

The monarch had sweeping power in the overseas territories, with not just sovereignty over the realm but also property rights. All power over the state came from the monarch. The crown had sweeping powers over the Catholic Church in its overseas territories, and via the [Patronato real](/wiki/Patronato_real "Patronato real"), a grant by the papacy to the crown to oversee the Church in all aspects save doctrine. The Viceroyalty of New Spain was created by royal decree on 12 October 1535, in the Kingdom of New Spain with a [viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") appointed as the king's "deputy" or substitute. This was the first New World viceroyalty and one of only two that the Spanish Empire administered in the continent until the 18th-century [Bourbon Reforms](/wiki/Bourbon_Reforms "Bourbon Reforms").

### Territorial extent of the overseas Spanish Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Territorial extent of the overseas Spanish Empire")]

Main article: [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Gastaldi_Nueva_Hispania_Tabula_Nova_1548_UTA.jpg/250px-Gastaldi_Nueva_Hispania_Tabula_Nova_1548_UTA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gastaldi_Nueva_Hispania_Tabula_Nova_1548_UTA.jpg)

[Giacomo Gastaldi](/wiki/Giacomo_Gastaldi "Giacomo Gastaldi")'s 1548 map of New Spain, *Nueva Hispania Tabula Nova*

At its greatest extent, the Spanish crown claimed on the mainland of the Americas much of North America south of Canada, that is: all of modern Mexico and Central America except [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama"); most of the United States west of the Mississippi River, plus [the Floridas](/wiki/Spanish_Florida "Spanish Florida"). The [Spanish West Indies](/wiki/Spanish_West_Indies "Spanish West Indies"), settled prior to the conquest of the Aztec Empire, also came under New Spain's jurisdiction: Cuba, [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola"), Puerto Rico, Jamaica, the [Cayman Islands](/wiki/Cayman_Islands "Cayman Islands"), [Trinidad](/wiki/Trinidad "Trinidad"), [Martinica](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique") and the [Bay Islands](/wiki/Bay_Islands_(department) "Bay Islands (department)").[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16) New Spain also claimed jurisdiction over the overseas territories of the [Spanish East Indies](/wiki/Spanish_East_Indies "Spanish East Indies") in Asia and Oceania: the Philippine Islands, the [Mariana Islands](/wiki/Mariana_Islands "Mariana Islands"), the [Caroline Islands](/wiki/Caroline_Islands "Caroline Islands"), parts of [Taiwan](/wiki/Spanish_Formosa "Spanish Formosa"), and parts of the [Moluccas](/wiki/Moluccas "Moluccas"). Although asserting sovereignty over this vast realm, it did not effectively control large swaths. Other European powers, including England, France, and the Netherlands established colonies in territories Spain claimed.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Spanish_North_America.png/250px-Spanish_North_America.png)](/wiki/File:Spanish_North_America.png)

Spanish historical presence, claimed territories, and expeditions in North America

Much of what was called in the United States the "Spanish borderlands" is territory that attracted few Spanish settlers, with less dense indigenous populations and apparently lacking in mineral wealth. Huge deposits of gold in California were discovered immediately after it was incorporated into the U.S. following the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") (1846–1848). The northern region of New Spain in the colonial era was considered marginal to Spanish interests compared to the most densely populated and lucrative areas of central Mexico. To shore up its claims in North America in the eighteenth century as other powers encroached on its claims, the crown sent [expeditions to the Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Spanish_expeditions_to_the_Pacific_Northwest "Spanish expeditions to the Pacific Northwest"), which explored and claimed the coast of British Columbia and Alaska.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

Religious missions and fortified presidios were established to shore up Spanish control on the ground. On the mainland, the administrative units included [Las Californias](/wiki/Las_Californias "Las Californias"), that is, the Baja California peninsula, still part of Mexico and divided into [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") and [Baja California Sur](/wiki/Baja_California_Sur "Baja California Sur"); [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California") (modern [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), western [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), and southern [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming")); (from the 1760s) [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_Spain) "Louisiana (New Spain)") (including the western Mississippi River basin and the Missouri River basin); [Nueva Extremadura](/wiki/Nueva_Extremadura "Nueva Extremadura") (the modern states of [Coahuila](/wiki/Coahuila "Coahuila") and [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")); and [Santa Fe de Nuevo México](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México") (parts of [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") and [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico")).[[16]](#cite_note-18)

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Government")]

Main article: [Spanish colonization of the Americas § Civil governance](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas#Civil_governance "Spanish colonization of the Americas")

#### Viceroyalty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Viceroyalty")]

The Viceroyalty was administered by a [viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") residing in Mexico City and appointed by the [Spanish monarch](/wiki/Spanish_monarchy "Spanish monarchy"), who had administrative oversight of all of these regions, although most matters were handled by the local governmental bodies, which ruled the various regions of the viceroyalty. First among these were the *[audiencias](/wiki/Audiencia_Real "Audiencia Real")*, which were primarily superior tribunals, but which also had administrative and legislative functions. Each of these was responsible to the Viceroy of New Spain in administrative matters (though not in judicial ones), but they also answered directly to the [Council of the Indies](/wiki/Council_of_the_Indies "Council of the Indies").

#### Captaincies general and governorates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Captaincies general and governorates")]

The [Captaincy Generals](/wiki/Captaincy_General "Captaincy General") were the second-level [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") and these were relatively [autonomous](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy") from the viceroyalty. The viceroy was captain-general of those provinces that remained directly under his command. [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Santo_Domingo "Captaincy General of Santo Domingo") (1535); [Philippines](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_the_Philippines "Captaincy General of the Philippines") (1565); [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Puerto_Rico "Captaincy General of Puerto Rico") (1580); [Cuba](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Cuba "Captaincy General of Cuba") (1608); [Guatemala](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Guatemala "Captaincy General of Guatemala") (1609); [Yucatán](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Yucat%C3%A1n "Captaincy General of Yucatán") (1617); [Commandancy General of the Provincias Internas](/wiki/Provincias_Internas "Provincias Internas") (1776) (analogous to a dependent captaincy general). Two [governorates](/wiki/Governorate#Spanish_Empire "Governorate"), third-level administrative divisions, were established, the Governorate of [Spanish Florida](/wiki/Spanish_Florida "Spanish Florida") (Spanish: *La Florida*) and the Governorate of [Spanish Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_Spain) "Louisiana (New Spain)") (Spanish: *Luisiana*).

#### Cartography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cartography")]

Spanish officials in New Spain used cartography as a tool to assert political authority. Maps served to define territorial claims and impose order on Indigenous communities, as mapmaking became closely tied to imperial governance. This shaped how land was conceptualized and controlled across the colony.[[17]](#cite_note-19)

#### High courts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: High courts")]

The high courts, or *audiencias*, were established in major areas of Spanish settlement. In New Spain the high court was established in 1527, prior to the establishment of the viceroyalty. The First Audiencia was headed by [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés")'s rival [Nuño de Guzmán](/wiki/Nu%C3%B1o_de_Guzm%C3%A1n "Nuño de Guzmán"), who used the court to deprive Cortés of power and property. The crown dissolved the First Audiencia and established the Second Audiencia.[[18]](#cite_note-20)
The audiencias of New Spain were [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Real_Audiencia_of_Santo_Domingo "Real Audiencia of Santo Domingo") (1511, effective 1526, predated the Viceroyalty); [Mexico](/wiki/Real_Audiencia_of_Mexico "Real Audiencia of Mexico") (1527, predated the Viceroyalty); [Panama](/wiki/Real_Audiencia_of_Panama "Real Audiencia of Panama") (1st one, 1538–1543); [Guatemala](/wiki/Real_Audiencia_of_Guatemala "Real Audiencia of Guatemala") (1543); [Guadalajara](/wiki/Real_Audiencia_of_Guadalajara "Real Audiencia of Guadalajara") (1548); [Manila](/wiki/Real_Audiencia_of_Manila "Real Audiencia of Manila") (1583).

Audiencia districts further incorporated the older, smaller divisions known as [governorates](/wiki/Governorate "Governorate") (*gobernaciones*, roughly equivalent to [provinces](/wiki/Provinces "Provinces")), which had been originally established by [conquistador](/wiki/Conquistador "Conquistador")-governors known as *[adelantados](/wiki/Adelantado "Adelantado")*. Provinces which were under military threat were grouped into [captaincies general](/wiki/Captaincy "Captaincy"), such as the Captaincies General of the Philippines (established 1574) and Guatemala (established in 1609), which were joint military and political commands with a certain level of autonomy. The viceroy was captain-general of those provinces that remained directly under his command.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Nocnopil_Joseph_de_Yturrigaray.png/250px-Nocnopil_Joseph_de_Yturrigaray.png)](/wiki/File:Nocnopil_Joseph_de_Yturrigaray.png)

Official document of the New Spain government in [Nahuatl](/wiki/Nahuatl "Nahuatl") announcing the transfer of powers from the outgoing viceroy [Félix Berenguer de Marquina](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Berenguer_de_Marquina "Félix Berenguer de Marquina") to [José de Iturrigaray](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_Iturrigaray "José de Iturrigaray"), who would serve until his arrest for his support of popular sovereignty in 1808

#### Local-level administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Local-level administration")]

At the local level there were over two hundred districts, in both indigenous and Spanish areas, which were headed by either a *[corregidor](/wiki/Corregidor_(position) "Corregidor (position)")* (also known as an *alcalde mayor*) or a *[cabildo](/wiki/Cabildo_(council) "Cabildo (council)")* (town council), both of which had judicial and administrative powers. In the late 18th century the [Bourbon dynasty](/wiki/Bourbon_dynasty "Bourbon dynasty") began phasing out the *corregidores* and introduced [intendants](/wiki/Intendant "Intendant"), whose broad fiscal powers cut into the authority of the viceroys, governors and *cabildos*. Despite their late creation, these intendancies so affected the formation of regional identity that they became the basis for the nations of Central America and the first [Mexican states](/wiki/Mexican_states "Mexican states") after [independence](/wiki/Mexican_independence "Mexican independence").

#### Intendancies of the 1780s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Intendancies of the 1780s")]

As part of the sweeping eighteenth-century administrative and economic changes known as the [Bourbon Reforms](/wiki/Bourbon_Reforms "Bourbon Reforms"), the Spanish crown created new administrative units called [intendancies](/wiki/Intendant_(government_official) "Intendant (government official)"), to strengthen central control over the viceroyalty. Some measures aimed to break the power of local elites in order to improve the economy of the empire. Reforms included the improvement of public participation in communal affairs, distribution of undeveloped lands to the indigenous and Spaniards, ending the corrupt practices of local crown officials, encouraging trade and mining, and establishing a system of territorial division similar to the model created by the government of France, already adopted in Spain.[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-22)

The establishment of intendancies was strongly resisted by the viceroyalties and general captaincies similar to the opposition in the Iberian Peninsula when the reform was adopted. Royal audiencias and ecclesiastical hierarchs opposed the reform for its intervention in economic issues, for its centralist politics, and the forced ceding of many of their functions to the intendants. In New Spain, these units generally corresponded to the regions or provinces that had developed earlier in the center, South, and North.[[21]](#cite_note-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24)

Many of the intendancy boundaries became Mexican state boundaries after independence. The intendancies were created between 1764 and 1789, with the greatest number in the mainland in 1786: 1764 La Habana (later subdivided); 1766 Nueva Orleans; 1784 Puerto Rico; 1786 México, Veracruz, Puebla de Los Ángeles, Guadalajara, Guanajuato, Zacatecas, San Luis Potosí, Sonora, Durango, Oaxaca, Guatemala, San Salvador, Comayagua, León, Santiago de Cuba, Puerto Príncipe; 1789 Mérida.[[23]](#cite_note-25)[[24]](#cite_note-26)

#### Social Hierarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Social Hierarchy")]

The social structure of New Spain was organized around a hierarchy that placed peninsulares and criollos at the top, while Indigenous peoples, Africans, and mixed-race groups faced restrictions on mobility and rights. Over time, there were demographic shifts and economic changes that challenged the hierarchy and created new opportunities for social advancement among certain groups.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

#### Legal Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Legal Culture")]

By the eighteenth century, New Spain had developed a distinctive legal culture that blended Spanish judicial traditions with local practices and regional autonomy. Courts relied on a wide array of legal documents and negotiations that allowed Indigenous communities and settlers to assert rights and/or resolve conflicts.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

## History of New Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: History of New Spain")]

Main article: [History of New Spain](/wiki/History_of_New_Spain "History of New Spain")

The history of mainland New Spain spans three hundred years from the [Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Aztec_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire") (1519–1521) to the collapse of Spanish rule in the [Mexican War of Independence](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence") (1810–1821).

Beginning with the Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire in 1521 by [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés"), Spanish rule was established, leading to the creation of governing bodies like the [Council of the Indies](/wiki/Council_of_the_Indies "Council of the Indies") and the [Audiencia](/wiki/Real_Audiencia_of_Mexico "Real Audiencia of Mexico") to maintain control. It involved the forced conversion of indigenous populations to Catholicism and the blending of Spanish and indigenous cultures in both the mainland and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") islands of [Martinica](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique") and [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola").

During the 17th and 18th centuries Spanish settlers founded major cities such as Mexico City, [Puebla](/wiki/Puebla "Puebla"), and [Guadalajara](/wiki/Guadalajara "Guadalajara"), turning New Spain into a vital part of the Spanish Empire. The discovery of silver in [Zacatecas](/wiki/Zacatecas "Zacatecas") and [Guanajuato](/wiki/Guanajuato "Guanajuato") significantly boosted the economy, leading to conflicts like the [Chichimeca War](/wiki/Chichimeca_War "Chichimeca War"). Missions and presidios were established in northern frontiers, aiding in the expansion and control of territories that later became part of the southwestern United States. The 18th century saw the implementation of the [Bourbon Reforms](/wiki/Bourbon_Reforms "Bourbon Reforms"), which aimed to modernize and strengthen the colonial administration and economy. These reforms included the creation of [intendancies](/wiki/Intendant "Intendant"), increased military presence, and the centralization of royal authority. The expulsion of the [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") and the establishment of economic societies, were part of the efforts to enhance efficiency and revenue for the crown.

The decline of New Spain culminated in the early 19th century with the Mexican War of Independence. Following [Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla](/wiki/Miguel_Hidalgo_y_Costilla "Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla")'s 1810 [Cry of Dolores](/wiki/Cry_of_Dolores "Cry of Dolores"), the insurgent army waged an eleven-year war against Spanish rule. The eventual alliance between royalist military officer [Agustín de Iturbide](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_de_Iturbide "Agustín de Iturbide") and insurgent leader [Vicente Guerrero](/wiki/Vicente_Guerrero "Vicente Guerrero") led to the successful campaign for independence. In 1821, New Spain officially became the independent nation of Mexico, ending three centuries of Spanish colonial rule. During a period that was especially important to the social development of America, the movement to expel the Moors and Jews from Spain stirred up a previously dormant aspect of Spanish social structure.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Spain&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economic history of Mexico § Economic history of New Spain, 1521-1821](/wiki/Economic_history_of_Mexico#Economic_history_of_New_Spain,_1521-1821 "Economic history of Mexico")
